law_citations: Art. 59 Abs. 2 lit. a und b ZPO
test_001.reasoning: The cited provisions cover (1) the requirements and procedure for interim relief (Art. 261–264 ZPO), including jurisdiction (Art. 65 ZPO), standing (Art. 59–60 ZPO), and legal aid (Art. 64 ZPO); (2) international jurisdiction and applicable law for foreign claimants and acts with cross-border elements (Art. 10, 109, 110 IPRG); (3) substantive copyright law (Art. 2, 9, 62, 66, 98 URG), unfair competition and trade secret protection (Art. 5, 9, 15 UWG), and civil liability for breach of fiduciary duty (Art. 423 OR); (4) personality rights (Art. 28 ZGB) as a possible basis for injunctive relief; (5) constitutional guarantees of access to justice and due process (Art. 6, 29, 29a BV); (6) Federal Supreme Court appeal requirements (Art. 72, 74, 75, 100 BGG); and (7) cost allocation (Art. 106 ZPO). The selected BGE and recent decisions clarify the requirements for interim relief, plausibility of rights, standing of foreign claimants, and the application of copyright and unfair competition law to software and trade secrets.

test_002.reasoning: The cited legal provisions cover the core substantive law (SVG and OR) governing liability for motor vehicle accidents (Art. 58, 59, 63, 65, 72, 83 SVG), the calculation and limitation of damages (Art. 41, 42, 44, 46, 47, 49 OR), and the prescription (Art. 60 OR, Art. 83 SVG). Art. 8 ZGB is cited for the burden of proof, and Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV for the right to be heard, especially regarding the use of medical evidence. The procedural articles (BGG, ZPO) are standard for civil appeals, addressing admissibility, deadlines, and costs. The BGE and recent unreported cases are leading authorities on the prescription of claims under SVG, the requirements for gross negligence, and the application of Art. 59 SVG. These cases clarify when the prescription period begins (knowledge of damage and causality), the legal standard for gross negligence in traffic accidents, and the exoneration of the vehicle holder. The cited cases and articles together provide the legal framework and jurisprudential context for determining both the timeliness of the additional claims and the potential exoneration of the vehicle holder.

test_003.reasoning: The cited articles of the Swiss Code of Obligations (OR) cover the requirements for contract formation, modification, and remission (Art. 1, 2, 16, 18, 115, 116, 143, 144 OR), as well as the rules for joint and several liability. The provisions on lease contracts (Art. 257f, 259a, 259b, 267, 267a, 266k OR) address the lessor's obligations, the lessee's liability for damages, and the notification of defects upon return. Art. 394 OR is included for the possible analogy to mandate contracts. Art. 97 OR covers damages for breach of contract. Art. 8 ZGB concerns the burden of proof. The KKG (LCC) articles clarify the scope of the Consumer Credit Act, which is excluded for business leases (Art. 1, 2 KKG). Procedural articles (BGG, ZPO) are standard for Federal Supreme Court jurisdiction, admissibility, and cost allocation. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV ensures the right to be heard. The BGE and recent unreported cases cited are leading authorities on remission of debt, contract modification, notification of defects, and the application of Art. 267a OR to lease contracts, as well as the exclusion of the KKG for business transactions. The cited cases also clarify the requirements for an effective remission and the necessity of acceptance, as well as the consequences of delayed notification of defects.

test_004.reasoning: The core issue is the calculation and suspension of the peremptory 15-month period under Art. 166 Abs. 2 SchKG for filing a bankruptcy petition after the lifting of opposition, and whether the period resumes upon notification of the operative dispositive or only after the reasoned decision. BGE 139 III 120 and BGE 138 III 483 are leading cases on the computation and suspension of this period, including the effect of appeals and notification of decisions. Art. 174 SchKG governs appeals against bankruptcy orders and the requirements for contesting solvency, with BGE 128 III 101 and BGE 132 III 140 addressing the evidentiary value of a debt enforcement register extract. Art. 80, 82, 175 SchKG and related provisions set the procedural framework for bankruptcy and opposition proceedings. Art. 29 Abs. 1 BV is cited for due process in notification. The cited BGG and ZPO provisions cover the Supreme Court's jurisdiction, deadlines, and procedural requirements. The recent unreported cases reflect current application of these principles in similar fact patterns.

test_006.reasoning: The PrHG (Federal Act on Product Liability) is central: Art. 1 PrHG defines scope, Art. 2 Abs. 1 PrHG defines 'product', Art. 3 PrHG defines 'producer', Art. 4 PrHG sets out the defect criterion, and Art. 5 PrHG (especially lit. b and e) provides exoneration grounds. Art. 6 and 7 PrHG cover damages and limitation periods. The burden of proof is governed by Art. 8 ZGB (general civil burden of proof), and the claim may also touch on tort (Art. 41 OR) or contractual liability (Art. 97 OR), with limitation periods (Art. 60 Abs. 1 OR, Art. 127 OR, Art. 130 Abs. 1 OR). Art. 135 ZPO and Art. 106 ZPO address costs and legal aid. Procedural BGG provisions (Art. 72, 74, 75, 76, 100) are standard for civil appeals. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV ensures the right to be heard. The cited BGE and unreported cases are leading authorities on product liability, burden of proof, exoneration, and limitation periods under the PrHG and OR, including the evidentiary requirements for defectiveness and the application of exculpatory clauses.

test_007.reasoning: The legal characterization of medical treatment as a contract of mandate (Art. 394 OR) versus a work contract is a classic issue in Swiss law, with the Federal Supreme Court consistently holding that most medical services, especially those involving ongoing care and professional judgment, are governed by mandate law (BGE 138 III 377 E. 4.2; BGE 132 III 359 E. 4.1). The physician's duty of care and liability for breach thereof are governed by Art. 398 Abs. 2 OR (duty to exercise due diligence of the profession), with liability for damages under Art. 97 OR and causation/damages rules (Art. 41, 42, 43, 44 OR). The burden of proof (Art. 8 ZGB), the rules for restitution of unjust enrichment (Art. 62, 63 OR), and the right to compensation for partial performance (Art. 404 OR; BGE 116 II 519 E. 3a) are also relevant. The patient's right to terminate the mandate at any time (Art. 404 OR) and the consequences for remuneration (BGE 120 II 248 E. 2a) are central to the counterclaim. Procedural citations include the relevant BGG and ZPO provisions for civil appeals, costs, and fundamental procedural rights (Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV). Leading and recent BGE and unreported decisions address the distinction between mandate and work contract, the duty of care in medical mandates, and the consequences of incomplete or defective performance.

test_008.error: Unterminated string starting at: line 327 column 5 (char 8524)

test_009.reasoning: The question concerns the recognition and enforceability in Switzerland of foreign probate and marital status decisions, especially in light of possible bigamy (ordre public) and formal defects (apostille). The core provisions are in the Swiss Private International Law Act (IPRG), notably Arts. 25, 27, 29 (general recognition, ordre public, and formal requirements), and Arts. 65, 96, 97 (status and succession). ZGB provisions on marital status, succession, and heirs' rights are relevant, as are BankG provisions on banking secrecy and disclosure. Constitutional rights (BV) may be invoked regarding equal treatment and due process. The BGG articles cited are standard for civil appeals (jurisdiction, standing, deadlines, costs). Leading BGE and recent 5A-series cases address recognition of foreign decisions, ordre public, bigamy, and banking disclosure. The cited cases and statutes cover all relevant legal and procedural aspects for a decision on this question.

test_010.reasoning: The legal question concerns whether the accused can be convicted as a co-author of qualified robbery (Art. 140 Abs. 1 und 3 StGB), rather than as a mere participant in theft (Art. 139 StGB), given the facts (weapons, violence, group action, and the accused's role). The cited StGB articles cover robbery, qualified robbery, theft, participation, co-authorship, attempt, and duress. The StPO articles address appellate review of facts and evidence (Art. 398 StPO), the principle in dubio pro reo (Art. 10 StPO), and procedural rules for appeals, costs, and legal aid. Constitutional and ECHR provisions (Art. 31, 32 BV; Art. 6 EMRK) ensure due process and presumption of innocence. The BGE and recent unreported cases cited are leading authorities on the distinction between co-authorship and participation, the requirements for qualified robbery, the in dubio pro reo principle, and the scope of appellate review under Art. 398 StPO. These citations collectively provide the legal framework and jurisprudence necessary for the Federal Supreme Court to answer the question.

test_011.reasoning: The legal question concerns international civil jurisdiction (ratione loci) of Swiss courts in a contractual dispute involving a foreign company, an assignment of claims, and the enforceability of a foreign forum-selection clause. The IPRG (LDIP) governs international jurisdiction and the recognition of foreign jurisdiction clauses (Art. 5, 112-114, 116, 17-19, 149 IPRG). The ZPO governs Swiss civil procedure (Art. 29, 59, 63, 106 ZPO). The OR provisions on agency without authority (Art. 38, 39 OR) and assignment (Art. 164, 167, 168 OR) are relevant to the substantive legal relationship and standing. The BV (Swiss Constitution) is cited for guarantees of equality and due process (Art. 8, 29 BV). The BGG provisions are cited for the admissibility and scope of appeal (Art. 75, 76, 100, 105 BGG). Leading BGE and recent unreported decisions are cited for the interpretation of forum-selection clauses, assignment of claims, and the doctrine of agency without authority.

test_012.reasoning:
The legal question involves (1) the characterisation of the contract (factoring vs. mixed contract: mandate, loan, current account), (2) the impact of that characterisation on prescription (Verjährung), (3) the burden of proof and accounting duties (Art. 400 OR), and (4) the effect of a written acknowledgement/novation (Schuldanerkennung/Novation) in the face of ongoing contestation. 

Substantive citations include core provisions on contract formation (Art. 1, 18 OR), mandate (Art. 394 ff. OR, esp. Art. 400 OR), loan (Art. 312 ff. OR), current account (Art. 487 ff. OR), and cooperative law (Art. 530 ff. OR). Prescription rules (Art. 127, 128, 130, 137, 139 OR) are central, as is the burden of proof (Art. 8 ZGB) and good faith (Art. 2 ZGB). The Federal Constitution (Art. 8, 29 BV) is cited for equality and due process. 

Procedural citations cover civil appeals to the Federal Supreme Court (Art. 72, 74, 75, 76, 100 BGG), cost allocation (Art. 106 ZPO), and legal aid (Art. 117 ZPO). 

Leading BGE and recent unreported cases are cited for contract characterisation (factoring, mandate, current account), prescription issues, the effect of acknowledgements/novation, and the duty to render accounts. The cited BGE and recent 4A decisions are standard authorities for these contract and prescription issues.

law_citations: Art. 1 Abs. 1 AVE-Verordnung zum GAV Personalverleih
Art. 2 Abs. 1 AVE-Verordnung zum GAV Personalverleih
Art. 1 Abs. 1 Kantonale Mindestlohnregelung (z.B. Art. 1 Abs. 1 Gesetz über Mindestlöhne im Kanton Zürich, falls einschlägig)
test_013.reasoning: The cited provisions cover: (1) the legal framework for collective labour agreements (OR 357 ff.), their extension (OR 360a, 360b), and their binding effect on non-members; (2) the EntsG and AVE-Verordnung, which regulate the extension and enforcement of collective agreements and minimum wage standards for posted and temporary workers; (3) the specific articles of the GAV Personalverleih (Swissstaffing/Unia) and its AVE-Verordnung, which set wage floors and refer to cantonal customary wages in certain branches; (4) cantonal minimum wage laws, which may supplement federal provisions; (5) constitutional rights (BV) relevant to equal treatment, economic freedom, and federal supremacy; (6) procedural rules for appeals to the Federal Supreme Court (BGG, ZPO); and (7) leading and recent Federal Supreme Court decisions interpreting the binding effect of collective agreements, the application of minimum wage clauses, the interplay between federal, cantonal, and contractual wage rules, and the rights of non-unionised temporary workers. The cited BGE and unreported cases are leading authorities on these issues.

test_014.reasoning: The cited legal provisions cover the definition of 'accident' (Art. 4 ATSG), the entitlement to accident insurance benefits (Art. 6 UVG), and the assimilation of certain lesions to accidents (Art. 6 Abs. 2 UVG). The ATSG and UVG articles cited are standard in accident insurance cases, especially those involving disputed causality and the distinction between accident and illness. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on the definition of accident, the requirements for proof, the assessment of medical evidence, and the assimilation of certain injuries to accidents. Recent unreported cases are included to reflect current jurisprudence on similar factual patterns, especially regarding contradictory statements, medical findings, and the burden of proof. Procedural articles from the BGG and ATSG are included for jurisdiction, admissibility, and standard of review. Constitutional articles (Art. 29 BV) are cited for due process and right to be heard, which are often invoked in cases with evidentiary disputes.

test_015.reasoning: The cited ZGB (Swiss Civil Code) articles cover the marital property regime (separation of property), the obligations between spouses, and the rules for divorce and post-divorce maintenance (Art. 125 ff. ZGB). The OR (Swiss Code of Obligations) articles address the law of mandate (Art. 394 ff. OR), simple partnership (Art. 530 ff. OR), unjust enrichment (Art. 62 OR), and tort (Art. 41 OR), all of which are relevant to the legal characterisation of the transfer and the wife's restitution claim. Art. 8 ZGB is cited for the burden of proof. Art. 2 ZGB (good faith) and Art. 29 ZGB (prescription) are often cited in such contexts. The ZPO (Swiss Civil Procedure Code) articles cited relate to divorce procedure, maintenance, and cost allocation. The BGG (Federal Supreme Court Act) articles cited are standard for civil appeals (jurisdiction, admissibility, deadlines, scope of review). Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (Federal Constitution) is included for the right to be heard. The cited BGE and unreported decisions are leading and recent cases on the legal characterisation of asset transfers between spouses, restitution claims, mandate vs. simple partnership, and post-divorce maintenance, especially for older spouses who gave up work for the marriage. These citations reflect the full legal framework and case law the Federal Supreme Court would use to resolve the complex factual and legal issues in this scenario.

test_016.reasoning: The cited ZGB provisions (Art. 176, 163, 125, 285) govern maintenance during separation, calculation of contributions, and the principle of mutual support. Art. 176 Abs. 3 ZGB and related BGE clarify the method for determining disposable income for self-employed persons, including whether to average profits or use the most recent year, and which charges are deductible. The cited BGE and recent cases address the treatment of personal withdrawals, incompressible expenses (mortgage, taxes, insurance), and the exclusion of non-essential costs (e.g., secondary residence). The imputation of hypothetical income to the non-earning spouse (Morgan) is addressed in BGE and recent 5A decisions. ZPO and BGG articles cover the procedure for provisional measures, appeals, and cost allocation. Constitutional articles (Art. 8, 9, 29 BV) ensure equal treatment, good faith, and due process. The cited cases are leading and recent authorities on maintenance calculation for self-employed payers and the imputation of hypothetical income.

test_017.reasoning: The cited OR (Swiss Code of Obligations) provisions cover the landlord's right to terminate for arrears (Art. 257d OR), the requirements for formal notice and cure period, and the rules for terminating commercial leases (Art. 266l OR, etc.). ZPO (Swiss Civil Procedure Code) articles cited include those governing the summary eviction ('cas clairs', Art. 257 ZPO), the requirements for summary proceedings, the introduction of nova (new evidence) on appeal (Art. 317 ZPO), and cost allocation (Art. 106 ZPO). The cited BV (Swiss Constitution) articles ensure due process and equal treatment. BGG articles establish the Federal Supreme Court's jurisdiction and procedural requirements. The BGE and recent unreported decisions cited are leading cases on eviction for arrears, the 'cas clairs' procedure, the requirements for valid service of notices, and the admissibility of nova on appeal. These citations reflect the legal framework and jurisprudence the Federal Supreme Court would rely on to decide the validity of the termination, the summary eviction, and the admissibility and effect of new evidence on appeal.

test_018.reasoning: The cited IRSG (Federal Act on International Mutual Assistance in Criminal Matters) provisions govern the procedure for executing foreign judicial assistance requests, including rights of affected third parties (Art. 65, 80k, 80l, 80m, 80n, 80p IRSG). Art. 63 IRSG covers the right to refuse cooperation, particularly for business/trade secrets. Art. 29 BV (right to be heard, due process), Art. 9 BV (good faith), and relevant BGG provisions (appeal deadlines, admissibility, legal interest) are standard in such Federal Supreme Court reviews. Art. 321 StGB (professional secrecy) and Art. 17 BGFA (attorney secrecy) may be relevant if legal privilege is at issue. The cited BGE decisions establish (a) the immediate reviewability of evidence orders in assistance proceedings, (b) the consequences of omitting remedy instructions (Nachfrist, good faith), and (c) the requirement to hear affected parties before executing such orders. Recent unreported cases are included to reflect the latest jurisprudence on procedural rights and remedies in international assistance contexts.

test_019.reasoning: The cited provisions cover (1) international jurisdiction and recognition of foreign divorces under the Swiss Private International Law Act (IPRG), including public policy (ordre public) review (Art. 27 IPRG), and the requirements for recognition (Art. 65 IPRG), as well as the ability to grant provisional measures (Art. 62, 63, 64 IPRG, Art. 98, 176, 276, 280, 296, 297 ZPO). (2) The ZPO provisions regulate provisional measures in matrimonial matters, including spousal maintenance. (3) The BGG provisions govern the admissibility and scope of federal appeals. (4) Constitutional provisions (Art. 8, 9, 14, 29 BV) are relevant for equality, due process, and non-discrimination, especially in public policy review. (5) The cited BGE and unreported decisions are leading cases on recognition of foreign divorces, provisional measures during pending foreign proceedings, and ordre public in family law. (6) The Hague Conventions (HKsÜ) may be cited if the foreign divorce involves child protection or maintenance recognition. The cited cases and statutes reflect the Federal Supreme Court’s approach to these intertwined questions.

test_020.reasoning: The legal framework centers on the statutory building mortgage (Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht), which is governed by Art. 837 ff. ZGB, especially Art. 837 Abs. 1 Ziff. 3 ZGB (contractor’s right), Art. 839 (provisional registration), and Art. 841-842 ZGB (definitive registration and procedure). The contractor’s claim, the owner’s objections (delay, defects, excessive extras), and the project manager’s deductions all engage contract law (Werkvertrag) under Art. 363 ff. OR, including rules on defects (Art. 368 OR), additional work (Art. 374 OR), and claims for payment (Art. 82 OR, Art. 84 OR). Disputes about the account and the need for an expert appraisal invoke Art. 157, 158, 168, and 170 ZPO (evidence, expert opinions), as well as Art. 55 ZPO (allegation duty), Art. 221 ZPO (statement of claim), and Art. 261 ff. ZPO (provisional measures). Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (right to be heard) is relevant to the right to an expert appraisal. The cited BGE and unreported cases are leading and recent decisions on the requirements for definitive registration of the mortgage, the effect of disputed accounts, the need for expert evidence, and the treatment of extras and defects. Cost and appeal provisions (Art. 106 ZPO, Art. 308 ff. ZPO) are standard in such judgments.

test_021.reasoning: The legal question concerns the liability of a Swiss freight forwarder (Spediteur/commissionnaire de transport) for loss of goods handled by a sub‑forwarder, and whether the rules of mandate (Art. 398 Abs. 3 OR, Art. 399 Abs. 2 OR) or those of forwarding contract (Art. 440 ff. OR) apply. The key substantive provisions are from the Swiss Code of Obligations (OR): the general rules on mandate (Art. 394 ff. OR), the specific rules on forwarding contracts (Art. 440 ff. OR), and the general liability for auxiliaries (Art. 101 OR). Art. 97 OR is cited for general contractual liability. Art. 8 ZGB concerns the burden of proof. Art. 1 Abs. 1 IPRG is cited for potential international elements (Costa Rican sub‑forwarder). Procedural citations include the relevant BGG articles for a civil appeal (Art. 100, 72, 74, 42 BGG), cost allocation (Art. 106 ZPO), and the right to be heard (Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV). The cited BGE and unreported cases are leading decisions on the liability of forwarding agents, the distinction between mandate and forwarding contracts, and the application of Art. 398 Abs. 3 OR and Art. 101 OR to sub‑mandates and auxiliaries. Recent unreported cases (4A_497/2017, etc.) address similar forwarding and sub‑forwarder liability issues.

test_022.reasoning: The question concerns international jurisdiction of Swiss courts for provisional and protective measures in a cross-border family dispute, with pending foreign divorce proceedings and child relocation. Art. 46 IPRG and Art. 10 IPRG are central to jurisdiction and provisional measures in international family law. The 1996 Hague Convention (HKsÜ 1996) governs child protection jurisdiction and the perpetuatio fori principle (see especially Arts. 5, 7, 8, 9, 16, 23). Art. 133 and 176 ZGB provide the substantive Swiss law basis for child custody and protective measures for the marital union. ZPO provisions (Art. 296, 98, 106) govern summary proceedings and costs. BGG provisions (Art. 49, 72, 74, 75, 76, 100, 105) cover the Federal Supreme Court's jurisdiction, admissibility, and procedure. Constitutional guarantees (Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV, Art. 8 Abs. 1 BV) may be cited for due process and family protection. The cited BGE and recent 5A decisions are leading and recent cases on international child protection, provisional measures, perpetuatio fori, and the interface between IPRG and the 1996 Hague Convention.

test_023.reasoning: The decision would cite Art. 52 Abs. 1 AHVG as the core provision establishing liability of company organs for unpaid AHV contributions. Art. 81 and 82 AHVG, and Art. 70, 82 ATSG, regulate the procedure for issuing and contesting AHV contribution decisions and recovery. Art. 716a, 717, and 754 OR define the duties and liability of board members, relevant for the scope of L.'s responsibility. Art. 55 ZGB and Art. 38 ZGB relate to the legal personality and representation of the company, including after dissolution. Art. 13 HRegV governs the effect of entries and deletions in the commercial register. Art. 49 BV (federal law primacy), Art. 8 BV (equality), and Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (right to be heard) are relevant to L.'s constitutional claims. Art. 82, 86, 89, 100, 105, and 106 BGG are standard procedural provisions for social insurance appeals to the Federal Supreme Court. Art. 61 ATSG (lit. a, f, h) governs the procedure before the cantonal insurance tribunal, including the right to be heard and evidence. The cited BGE precedents clarify the liability regime under Art. 52 AHVG, the requirements for differentiated solidarity, the effect of deregistration, and the procedural rights of former board members. Recent unreported cases (9C_xxx/yyyy) address similar issues of director liability, service of decisions, and procedural guarantees.

test_024.reasoning: The cited ZGB articles cover the legal bases for maintenance (Art. 125, 133), emergency maintenance (Art. 176), and marital property division (Art. 197, 204, 205, 210). ZPO articles govern the procedure for divorce (Art. 272, 274, 277), fact-finding and evidence (Art. 296, 297), and cost allocation (Art. 106). Art. 8 ZGB is the general rule on the burden of proof, and Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (right to be heard) and Art. 9 BV (prohibition of arbitrariness) are relevant for the evidentiary and procedural guarantees. BGG articles regulate the appeal to the Federal Supreme Court (Art. 95, 97, 105, 100, 72, 74). The cited BGE decisions clarify the standard of proof in divorce and maintenance proceedings (e.g., BGE 138 III 217, 137 III 226), the distinction between summary and ordinary proceedings (BGE 128 III 411), the duty to clarify facts and take evidence (BGE 130 III 417, 127 III 136), and the requirements for deferring division of marital property (BGE 120 II 393, 115 II 440). Recent unreported decisions illustrate current application of these principles, especially regarding the standard of proof, evidentiary duties, and the admissibility of new requests on appeal.

test_025.reasoning: The core substantive law is the Swiss Civil Code (ZGB) on the ordinary marital property regime (Errungenschaftsbeteiligung), including provisions on the composition of marital property (Art. 197, 198, 200, 201 ZGB), division (Art. 205, 206, 207, 210–212 ZGB), and co-ownership (Art. 650, 651 ZGB). The question of whether a Swiss court can order the sale or attribution of a foreign immovable is governed by private international law (IPRG), especially Art. 54, 59, and 96 IPRG, as well as leading BGE on the limits of Swiss jurisdiction over foreign real estate (lex rei sitae). The evidentiary rules and presumptions for insurance indemnities and movables acquired during marriage are found in Art. 200, 201, 205 ZGB, and the general rules of evidence (Art. 8, 9 ZGB; Art. 272, 274, 280 ZPO). Art. 2 ZGB (good faith) and Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (right to be heard) are often cited in this context. Procedural rules for federal appeals (BGG) and cost/legal aid provisions are standard. The cited BGE and recent unreported cases are leading authorities on: (1) the division of foreign immovables in Swiss divorce proceedings; (2) the presumption and proof of marital property; (3) the treatment of insurance indemnities and movables; and (4) the sequence of partitioning co-ownership and marital property liquidation.

test_026.reasoning: The cited ZGB articles cover the legal bases for matrimonial property (Errungenschaftsbeteiligung), income from co-owned property, and the rules on maintenance (spousal and child). Art. 176 ZGB governs the use of the family home during separation. Art. 197 ff. ZGB regulates the division of property. Art. 285 ff. ZGB and Art. 276 ff. ZGB address child maintenance, including routine and extraordinary expenses. Art. 164 ZGB and Art. 163 ZGB concern the duty to contribute to family maintenance. Art. 49 OR may be cited for unjust enrichment if Jordan retained more than his share of the rent. The cited BV articles ensure the constitutional rights to equality, protection of family, and due process. ZPO articles cover standing, legal aid, and family law procedure. BGG articles are for Federal Supreme Court jurisdiction, deadlines, and admissibility. The leading BGE and recent 5A_ cases clarify the division of rental income from co-owned property, the requirements for post-divorce spousal maintenance, and the calculation of child maintenance, including extraordinary costs.

test_027.error: Unterminated string starting at: line 347 column 5 (char 9194)

test_028.reasoning: Art. 58 Abs. 1 OR is the core provision for strict liability of property owners for defective buildings. Art. 41 Abs. 1 OR (general tort), Art. 55 Abs. 1 OR (liability for auxiliaries), and Art. 97 Abs. 1 OR (contractual liability) are often referenced for context and to distinguish strict liability from fault-based liability. Art. 2 ZGB (good faith) and Art. 8 ZGB (burden of proof) are standard in civil liability cases. Art. 1 Abs. 2 ZGB is cited for analogy and legal development. Art. 16 SVG (road safety) may be cited in analogous safety contexts. Art. 4 Abs. 1 BV (equality before the law) and Art. 8 Abs. 1 BV (equality) may be referenced if discrimination issues arise. Art. 100 Abs. 1 BGG, Art. 42 Abs. 1 BGG, and Art. 105 Abs. 1 BGG are standard procedural citations for civil appeals. Art. 106 ZPO governs cost allocation. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV (right to be heard) is a standard constitutional guarantee. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on Art. 58 OR, construction defects, maintenance duties, and causation, including the effect of age and user conduct on liability. Recent unreported cases (4A series) address similar factual constellations, evidentiary issues, and the limits of owner liability.

test_029.reasoning: The case concerns adult protection under ZGB (Swiss Civil Code), specifically the opening of an inquiry (Art. 393 ZGB), the ordering of a psychiatric expert assessment (Art. 446, 447 ZGB), and the provisional appointment of a guardian (Art. 391, 398, 405 ZGB). The appealability of interim measures (such as expert assessments) is governed by Art. 93 BGG and related jurisprudence on irreparable harm. Fundamental rights (BV, EMRK) are implicated due to the significant interference with personal autonomy and privacy. Procedural provisions (BGG, ZPO) govern admissibility, deadlines, and legal aid. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on the requirements for guardianship, proportionality, necessity, and appealability of interim measures in adult protection. Recent unreported decisions are included for up-to-date application of these principles.

test_030.reasoning: The cited ZGB articles (Art. 176, 163, 164) govern protective measures in the matrimonial sphere, the obligation of spouses to provide maintenance, and the calculation of maintenance contributions. Art. 285 and 276 ZGB are relevant if child maintenance is at issue due to the presence of a non-common child. The cited ZPO provisions (Art. 272, 276, 296) regulate summary proceedings and the evidentiary regime in interim measures. Art. 106 ZPO covers cost allocation, and Art. 117 ZPO legal aid. The cited BGG articles establish the requirements and scope of Federal Supreme Court review in summary family law proceedings. Constitutional provisions (Art. 8, 9, 29 BV) ensure equal treatment, good faith, and due process. The cited BGE and unreported decisions are leading cases on imputation of hypothetical income (Erwerbsfähigkeit), the adaptation period (Übergangsfrist), calculation of the subsistence minimum (Existenzminimum), surplus allocation (Überschussverteilung), and the evidentiary requirements for maintenance and housing cost allocation. These cases clarify the principles for attributing notional income, the need for a reasonable adaptation period, and the methodology for dividing maintenance obligations and housing costs between spouses in interim proceedings.

test_031.reasoning: The core substantive law is Art. 125 ZGB (spousal maintenance after divorce), with Art. 125 Abs. 1-3 ZGB detailing the prerequisites, calculation, and duration. Art. 126 ZGB covers the form and duration of maintenance. Art. 163 and 164 ZGB (duty of support during marriage) and Art. 176 ZGB (measures during separation) are relevant for the historic standard of living and the transition from separation to divorce. The cited constitutional provisions (Art. 8, 9, 13, 29 BV) are often invoked in maintenance cases for equality, protection of private life, and procedural fairness. ZPO provisions (Art. 272, 276, 280, 117, 118, 106) govern the procedure for maintenance claims, legal aid, and cost allocation. The BGG articles cited are standard for civil appeals to the Federal Supreme Court. The leading BGE decisions clarify the principles for determining entitlement to maintenance, the calculation methodology (minimum subsistence vs. standard of living), and the impact of long marriages, health, and earning capacity. Recent unreported decisions are included to show current application of these principles.

test_032.reasoning: The cited provisions and cases cover the legal framework for ordering and maintaining pretrial detention (Art. 221 StPO, Art. 212 StPO), the requirement to assess less intrusive alternatives (Art. 237 StPO), and the constitutional and ECHR guarantees of personal liberty and proportionality (Art. 31 BV, Art. 10 BV, Art. 5 EMRK). Jurisdiction, admissibility, and procedural rules are included (Art. 393, 396, 382 StPO; Art. 100, 78, 80 BGG). Cost and legal aid provisions are standard. The BGE and recent unreported cases cited are leading authorities on the requirements for flight risk, the necessity and proportionality of detention, the assessment of alternatives, and the weighing of personal circumstances, including local ties and family situation. The cited BGE 137 IV 122 and others are seminal on flight risk and alternatives to detention. Recent unreported cases provide up-to-date application of these principles.

test_033.reasoning: The cited legal provisions cover the definition of occupational disease (Art. 9 UVG, Art. 6 UVG), the general concept of accident and disease under the ATSG, and the requirements for recognition of occupational diseases and aggravation of pre-existing conditions (Art. 36, 49, 50 UVV). Art. 44 ATSG and Art. 28 VwVG address the insurer's investigatory obligations and the requirements for expert medical examination. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV ensures the right to be heard and a sufficient investigation of the facts. The BGG and ATSG articles cited are standard for social insurance appeals, jurisdiction, and review. The cited BGE and recent unreported cases are leading decisions on the definition and proof of occupational diseases, the aggravation of pre-existing conditions, the standard of proof, and the insurer's duty to clarify the facts, including when expert opinions are required. These are all directly relevant to the assessment of whether granulomatosis with polyangiitis and aggravated asthma qualify as occupational diseases or aggravations under the UVG, and whether Omega Insurance fulfilled its investigatory duties.

test_034.reasoning: The core issue is the interpretation and application of Art. 839 ZGB, specifically the calculation of the four-month period for registration of the craftsmen's and contractors' lien (Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht). The cited ZGB provisions (Arts. 836–839, 963, 965, 971, 972, 974) cover the requirements, scope, and registration process for liens. OR provisions (Art. 18, Art. 55) are relevant for contract interpretation and liability. Art. 8 ZGB governs the burden of proof. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV is cited for the right to be heard in civil proceedings. The BGG and ZPO provisions are standard for civil appeals, deadlines, and cost allocation. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on the calculation of the four-month period (insbesondere BGE 144 III 43, BGE 132 III 281, BGE 129 III 169), the definition of 'definitive withdrawal' or 'cessation of work,' and the requirements for valid registration. Recent unreported decisions (5A_297/2021, 5A_1004/2020, etc.) reflect the latest jurisprudence on the timing and evidentiary requirements for the Bauhandwerkerpfandrecht.

test_035.reasoning: The core issue is whether, under Art. 263 ZPO (which governs the lapse of provisional measures if an action on the merits is not filed within the set deadline), a foreign merits proceeding (already pending and concerning the same subject matter) can satisfy the requirement to prevent the lapse of Swiss provisional measures. Art. 89 IPRG addresses international jurisdiction in inheritance matters, and Art. 92 IPRG deals with jurisdiction for provisional measures. Art. 264 ZPO and Art. 261 ZPO provide the general framework for provisional measures, while Art. 59 ZPO and Art. 88 ZPO concern admissibility and standing. Art. 8, 29, and 29a BV may be cited for procedural guarantees and equality before the law. Art. 54, 560, 566, and 571 ZGB are relevant for inheritance law, renunciation, and succession. Art. 74, 75, 76, 100, and 105 BGG are standard for civil appeals to the Federal Supreme Court. Art. 106 ZPO covers costs. The cited BGE and unreported cases address the requirements for 'action on the merits' in the context of provisional measures, the effect of foreign proceedings, and the relationship between Swiss and foreign litigation in inheritance and provisional measures contexts.

test_036.reasoning: The cited provisions and cases cover: (1) the legal basis for ordering DNA profiling (Art. 255 StPO), including the requirements for such measures (BGE 141 IV 454, BGE 140 IV 40); (2) the general conditions for coercive measures, including proportionality and necessity (Art. 197 StPO, BGE 137 IV 340, BGE 138 IV 81); (3) the competence and procedure for juvenile proceedings (JStPO provisions, BGE 144 IV 74); (4) the right to be heard and file access (Art. 101 StPO, Art. 29 BV, BGE 143 IV 380, 1B_172/2023); (5) the principle of proportionality and fundamental rights (Art. 36 BV, BGE 139 IV 128, BGE 143 IV 214); (6) procedural and cost rules (Art. 428 StPO, Art. 135 StPO); (7) the relevant BGG provisions for admissibility and review of the Federal Supreme Court; and (8) recent unreported cases addressing DNA profiling, proportionality, and the right to be heard in criminal and juvenile contexts.

test_037.reasoning: The cited articles of the Trademark Protection Act (MSchG) cover the requirements for trademark protection, the scope of protection (including for international marks), the rights conferred, and the remedies available (injunction, transfer of domain names). The Unfair Competition Act (UWG) provisions address acts of confusion, exploitation of reputation, and the general clause of unfairness, all relevant to domain name disputes and competitive conduct. The Code of Obligations (OR) and Civil Procedure Code (ZPO) provisions cited are relevant for company registration, standing, and procedural requirements. The Federal Supreme Court Act (BGG) articles govern admissibility, standing, and deadlines for appeals. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV ensures the right to be heard. The BGE and recent unreported cases are leading authorities on domain names, trademark infringement, unfair competition, and the transfer of domain names as a remedy, as well as the distinctiveness and confusion criteria. The cited cases are the main precedents on domain name disputes and the intersection of trademark and unfair competition law in Switzerland.

test_038.reasoning: The core legal basis for civil liability is Art. 41 Abs. 1 OR (unlawful act), with Art. 47 OR and Art. 49 Abs. 1 OR governing compensation for moral (non-material) damage. Art. 42 Abs. 1 OR and Art. 43 Abs. 1 OR regulate proof and calculation of damages, including lost earnings and medical costs. Art. 44 Abs. 1 OR allows for reduction of damages in case of contributory fault (e.g., claimant's stay abroad). Art. 8 ZGB governs the burden of proof, and Art. 328 Abs. 1 OR may be relevant if the employment relationship is discussed. Art. 29 Abs. 2 BV and Art. 9 BV are cited for fair proceedings and prohibition of arbitrariness. Procedural citations (Art. 100 Abs. 1 BGG, Art. 72 Abs. 1 BGG, Art. 74 Abs. 1 lit. b BGG, Art. 105 Abs. 1 BGG, Art. 106 ZPO, Art. 117 ZPO) cover appeal, jurisdiction, and cost/legal aid. The cited BGE decisions are leading cases on causation, proof of damages, moral damages, and the requirements for liability under Art. 41 OR. Recent unreported decisions (4A_240/2022, etc.) reflect current practice on moral damages and causation in tort cases.

test_039.reasoning: The legal characterization of the relationship (simple partnership, mandate, subcontracting, or sui generis) requires reference to the rules on simple partnership (Art. 530 ff. OR), mandate (Art. 394 ff. OR), and the principle of party intent (Art. 18 OR). The facts suggest a simple partnership per Art. 530 OR, but mandate and other forms must be excluded by reference to the parties' conduct and intent. Burden of proof for claims and for mistake/fraud is governed by Art. 8 ZGB and Art. 42 OR, with further procedural rules in Art. 55 ZPO. The rules on mistake and fraud (Art. 23, 24, 28 OR) are cited for C's challenge. Allocation of fees/expenses and accounting between partners is governed by Art. 533, 534, 535 OR. Limitation periods (Art. 127 OR) and unjust enrichment (Art. 62 OR) may arise if the contract is set aside. The cited BGE and recent cases clarify the criteria for partnership vs. mandate, the burden of proof, and the requirements for setting aside agreements for error or fraud. Procedural and constitutional articles (BGG, ZPO, BV) are standard in such civil appeals.

test_040.reasoning: The cited ZGB provisions cover the requirements for marriage, the effects of marriage, property regimes, maintenance obligations, and the rules on protective measures for the marital union (Art. 176 ZGB). Art. 2 Abs. 2 ZGB (abuse of rights) is central to the question of whether the maintenance claim is barred due to a sham marriage. The cited ZPO articles cover the summary procedure for protective measures, provisional legal aid (provisio ad litem), and cost allocation. The BGG provisions cited are standard for civil appeals, including standing, deadlines, and scope of review. Constitutional articles (Art. 8, 13, 29 BV) are included for equality, protection of private/family life, and due process, which may be invoked in such proceedings. The BGE and recent unreported decisions cited are leading cases on abuse of rights in marriage, sham marriages, maintenance during protective measures, and the assessment criteria under Art. 176 ZGB. These cases clarify the requirements for maintenance, the role of common life and mutual support, and the application of abuse of rights doctrine in marital disputes.
